title: Houses for Rent on Cleveland's East Side Under $1,000
metaTitle: East Side Cleveland Houses for Rent Under $1,000
metaDescription: Houses for rent on Cleveland's east side under $1,000/mo: realistic sizes, neighborhoods, ZIP codes, and transit. Section 8-friendly homes. Book a showing.
primaryKeyword: houses for rent east side cleveland under 1000
answerBox: A house for rent on Cleveland's east side under $1,000 a month is most realistic for a 1-bedroom or smaller 2-bedroom home; 3-bedroom east-side houses typically start just above $1,000. The east side spans Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax, and Hough, generally east of the Cuyahoga River along the RTA Red, Blue, and Green lines.

sections.h2: Is there a house for rent under $1,000 on Cleveland's east side?
body: Yes, and the east side is generally where under-$1,000 houses are easiest to find in Cleveland, though bedroom count still drives the price more than which side of town you're on. Zumper's July 4, 2026 report puts Cleveland's citywide median asking rent at $1,250/mo, with a 2-bedroom median around $1,100/mo. An under-$1,000 house is typically a smaller unit at the lower end of the market rather than a typical listing.

The east side covers 17 of the city's 34 official Statistical Planning Areas, including Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax, Hough, Buckeye-Shaker, St. Clair-Superior, Mount Pleasant, Union-Miles, and Lee-Harvard. Housing stock here is largely early-1900s single-family homes and duplexes with front porches, small yards, and full basements.

RentCafe's July 2, 2026 report on professionally-managed apartment complexes shows a much higher citywide average — $1,564/mo overall, with 2-bedroom units averaging $1,818/mo. That higher figure mainly reflects newer apartment buildings rather than the older houses and duplexes that make up most of the east side's under-$1,000 rental stock, so a citywide apartment average shouldn't discourage a house search here.

sections.h2: What size house fits under $1,000, by bedroom count
body: Here's a realistic breakdown based on rents across our own Cleveland-wide rental portfolio, which typically range from about $700 to $1,800 a month with a median near $1,000. Treat the top two rows as the more reliable bets for a firm $1,000 ceiling.

sections.h2: East side neighborhoods and ZIP codes to know
body: Slavic Village (ZIP 44105) runs along the Morgana Run Trail with commercial strips on Broadway and Fleet Avenues. Collinwood (44110), in the city's far northeast, includes the Waterloo Arts District. Glenville (44108) sits near University Circle and St. Clair Avenue. Fairfax (44104) is adjacent to the Cleveland Clinic's main campus, and Hough (44103) sits between Downtown and University Circle. Buckeye-Shaker (44120) borders Shaker Heights directly along the Shaker Boulevard rail corridor.

Housing stock varies a bit by ZIP. Slavic Village and Union-Miles further south tend to have more standalone duplexes on larger lots, while Hough and Fairfax, closer to University Circle and the Cleveland Clinic, mix in more rowhouse-style and multi-family buildings. As with the west side, the individual home's condition and size matter more for price than the specific neighborhood.

Farther out, Lee-Harvard and Lee-Seville sit near the city's southern boundary, Mount Pleasant and Kinsman fill in the area between Buckeye and Union-Miles, and Euclid-Green and North Shore Collinwood round out the far-northeast lakefront edge. Renters focused strictly on an under-$1,000 budget often do better casting a wide net across several of these ZIPs than fixating on one specific neighborhood.

For the broader east-side picture, see our page on [houses for rent on Cleveland's east side](/cleveland-rentals/houses/houses-for-rent-east-side-cleveland/), or drill into [houses for rent in Slavic Village](/cleveland-rentals/neighborhoods/houses-for-rent-slavic-village-cleveland/) for a single-neighborhood breakdown.

sections.h2: Getting around the east side on a tight budget
body: The RTA Red Line runs from downtown's Tower City hub out to Windermere station in East Cleveland, and the HealthLine bus rapid transit route runs along Euclid Avenue connecting Public Square to University Circle. The Blue and Green light-rail lines serve the Buckeye-Shaker corridor directly into downtown. Note that the eastern half of Cuyahoga County, including much of the east side, sits in the region's lake-effect "Snow Belt," so winter driving conditions can be more pronounced here than on the lakefront west side.

If you commute to the Cleveland Clinic, University Hospitals, or the University Circle institutions — among the region's largest employers — an east-side home near the Red Line, HealthLine, or Blue/Green corridors can cut a car-dependent commute down considerably. Cleveland Clinic ranks as the region's single largest employer, with University Hospitals close behind, and healthcare jobs make up a large share of the largest-employer list for Greater Cleveland overall, so a short commute to either campus is a real practical advantage for many east-side renters.

sections.h2: What to budget for beyond rent at this price point
body: At any price under $1,000, confirm exactly which utilities are included before signing. Most east-side rental houses do not include electric or gas in the rent. Electric service comes from either **Cleveland Public Power** or **The Illuminating Company** depending on the address, and natural gas typically comes from **Enbridge Gas Ohio**. Water usually runs through Cleveland Water, with sewer billing generally tied to the Northeast Ohio Regional Sewer District. Ask the landlord which utilities are your responsibility and, if possible, request a recent bill estimate.

Also budget for a security deposit. Under Ohio law (ORC 5321.16), any deposit over $50 or one month's rent, whichever is greater, must earn 5% annual interest if you stay six months or more, and the landlord must return your deposit with an itemized statement of deductions within 30 days of move-out, once you've provided a forwarding address.

We manage 90-plus rental homes across Greater Cleveland, and every one accepts Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8) and is HUD-inspection-ready, regardless of the sticker rent. If you hold a voucher, [CMHA](https://www.cmha.net/housing/applicants/housing_choice_vouchers.php), the Cuyahoga County housing authority, pays a subsidy up to a set payment standard for your bedroom size, which can bring your out-of-pocket cost below $1,000 even on a slightly higher-priced home; check CMHA's current payment-standard chart rather than assuming a fixed figure.

Keep in mind Ohio has no statewide source-of-income protection law, and the City of Cleveland itself does not require landlords to accept vouchers, though every home we manage does. A handful of nearby suburbs, including Cleveland Heights, South Euclid, University Heights, and Warrensville Heights, do have their own local voucher-protection ordinances — but those rules apply only within those specific municipalities, not citywide in Cleveland. [Book a free showing](/p/book-showing) and tell us your budget and preferred east-side neighborhood, or see our [Section 8 houses on the east side](/cleveland-rentals/section-8/section-8-houses-east-side-cleveland/) page for voucher-specific detail.

faq.q: Can I rent a house on Cleveland's east side for under $1,000 a month?
faq.a: Yes, most realistically a 1-bedroom or smaller 2-bedroom home. Zumper's July 4, 2026 report shows Cleveland's citywide 2-bedroom median at $1,100/mo, so an under-$1,000 2-bedroom tends to be smaller or older, not the typical unit. 3-bedroom and larger east-side houses usually start above $1,000, so staying flexible on size helps.

faq.q: Which east-side neighborhoods are worth checking for a house under $1,000?
faq.a: Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax, and Hough are the main east-side rental neighborhoods, spanning ZIP codes 44105, 44110, 44108, 44104, and 44103. Availability and price depend on the specific home's size, age, and condition rather than the neighborhood alone, so it helps to check listings across several of these ZIPs.

faq.q: Do you manage rental houses on Cleveland's east side?
faq.a: We manage 90-plus rental homes across Greater Cleveland, with meaningful inventory on the East and Southeast side. Book a free showing and tell us your target neighborhood, bedroom count, and budget, and we'll let you know what's currently open in that range.

faq.q: Does an under-$1,000 east-side rental accept Section 8 vouchers?
faq.a: Every home we manage accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready, regardless of the listed rent. With a voucher, CMHA's payment standard may cover part of the cost based on your household income, which can lower what you pay out of pocket well below the sticker price.

faq.q: What transit serves Cleveland's east side?
faq.a: The RTA Red Line runs from downtown's Tower City to Windermere station in East Cleveland, the HealthLine runs along Euclid Avenue to University Circle, and the Blue and Green light-rail lines serve the Buckeye-Shaker corridor into downtown for commuters without a car.
